1	# Fixture: heatmap
2	## Task
3	10x10 confusion matrix. Annotate diagonal with values, off-diagonal cells with a sequential cmap.
4	## Inputs
5	- data.json: {x_labels, y_labels, matrix} where matrix[i][j] = predictions of true class i as class j.
6	## Outputs
7	- figure.pdf, figure.png; caption.tex; gen_figure.py
8	## Hints
9	- Use a perceptually uniform colormap (viridis / inferno / Blues).
10	- Label rows and columns. Diagonal text should be readable on dark cells.
11

# Fixture: heatmap
## Task
10x10 confusion matrix. Annotate diagonal with values, off-diagonal cells with a sequential cmap.
## Inputs
- data.json: {x_labels, y_labels, matrix} where matrix[i][j] = predictions of true class i as class j.
## Outputs
- figure.pdf, figure.png; caption.tex; gen_figure.py
## Hints
- Use a perceptually uniform colormap (viridis / inferno / Blues).
- Label rows and columns. Diagonal text should be readable on dark cells.

delta.partial_json:
{"file_path": "/Users/mjm/MinionsOS/outline/ExperimentsOfMinionsos/FigureDraw/sessions/research-paper-writing/heatmap/20260524T151201Z/workspace/gen_figure.py", "content": "import json
import numpy as np
import matplotlib
matplotlib.use("Agg")
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as ticker

with open("data.json") as f:
    d = json.load(f)

matrix = np.array(d["matrix"])
x_labels = d["x_labels"]
y_labels = d["y_labels"]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 6))

# Off-diagonal: Blues colormap; diagonal will still use same cmap but text forced white/black
im = ax.imshow(matrix, cmap="Blues", aspect="equal")

# Colorbar
cbar = fig.colorbar(im, ax=ax, fraction=0.046, pad=0.04)
cbar.set_label("Count", fontsize=10)

# Annotate all cells; diagonal gets bold white text, off-diagonal gets standard text
vmax = matrix.max()
for i in range(matrix.shape[0]):
    for j in range(matrix.shape[1]):
        val = matrix[i, j]
        on_diag = (i == j)
        # Choose text color based on cell brightness
        normed = val / vmax
        text_color = "white" if normed > 0.55 else "black"
        weight = "bold" if on_diag else "normal"
        ax.text(j, i, str(val), ha="center", va="center",
                fontsize=8, color=text_color, fontweight=weight)

ax.set_xticks(range(len(x_labels)))
ax.set_xticklabels(x_labels, fontsize=9)
ax.set_yticks(range(len(y_labels)))
ax.set_yticklabels(y_labels, fontsize=9)
ax.set_xlabel("Predicted class", fontsize=10)
ax.set_ylabel("True class", fontsize=10)
ax.set_title("Confusion Matrix (10-class classifier)", fontsize=11, pad=10)

# Highlight diagonal with a thin border
for i in range(matrix.shape[0]):
    rect = plt.Rectangle((i - 0.5, i - 0.5), 1, 1,
                          linewidth=1.2, edgecolor="white", facecolor="none")
    ax.add_patch(rect)

plt.tight_layout()
fig.savefig("figure.pdf", dpi=300, bbox_inches="tight")
fig.savefig("figure.png", dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
fig.savefig("figure.svg", bbox_inches="tight")
print("Saved figure.pdf, figure.png, figure.svg")
"}

input.content:
import json
import numpy as np
import matplotlib
matplotlib.use("Agg")
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as ticker

with open("data.json") as f:
    d = json.load(f)

matrix = np.array(d["matrix"])
x_labels = d["x_labels"]
y_labels = d["y_labels"]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 6))

# Off-diagonal: Blues colormap; diagonal will still use same cmap but text forced white/black
im = ax.imshow(matrix, cmap="Blues", aspect="equal")

# Colorbar
cbar = fig.colorbar(im, ax=ax, fraction=0.046, pad=0.04)
cbar.set_label("Count", fontsize=10)

# Annotate all cells; diagonal gets bold white text, off-diagonal gets standard text
vmax = matrix.max()
for i in range(matrix.shape[0]):
    for j in range(matrix.shape[1]):
        val = matrix[i, j]
        on_diag = (i == j)
        # Choose text color based on cell brightness
        normed = val / vmax
        text_color = "white" if normed > 0.55 else "black"
        weight = "bold" if on_diag else "normal"
        ax.text(j, i, str(val), ha="center", va="center",
                fontsize=8, color=text_color, fontweight=weight)

ax.set_xticks(range(len(x_labels)))
ax.set_xticklabels(x_labels, fontsize=9)
ax.set_yticks(range(len(y_labels)))
ax.set_yticklabels(y_labels, fontsize=9)
ax.set_xlabel("Predicted class", fontsize=10)
ax.set_ylabel("True class", fontsize=10)
ax.set_title("Confusion Matrix (10-class classifier)", fontsize=11, pad=10)

# Highlight diagonal with a thin border
for i in range(matrix.shape[0]):
    rect = plt.Rectangle((i - 0.5, i - 0.5), 1, 1,
                          linewidth=1.2, edgecolor="white", facecolor="none")
    ax.add_patch(rect)

plt.tight_layout()
fig.savefig("figure.pdf", dpi=300, bbox_inches="tight")
fig.savefig("figure.png", dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
fig.savefig("figure.svg", bbox_inches="tight")
print("Saved figure.pdf, figure.png, figure.svg")

tool_use_result.content:
import json
import numpy as np
import matplotlib
matplotlib.use("Agg")
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as ticker

with open("data.json") as f:
    d = json.load(f)

matrix = np.array(d["matrix"])
x_labels = d["x_labels"]
y_labels = d["y_labels"]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 6))

# Off-diagonal: Blues colormap; diagonal will still use same cmap but text forced white/black
im = ax.imshow(matrix, cmap="Blues", aspect="equal")

# Colorbar
cbar = fig.colorbar(im, ax=ax, fraction=0.046, pad=0.04)
cbar.set_label("Count", fontsize=10)

# Annotate all cells; diagonal gets bold white text, off-diagonal gets standard text
vmax = matrix.max()
for i in range(matrix.shape[0]):
    for j in range(matrix.shape[1]):
        val = matrix[i, j]
        on_diag = (i == j)
        # Choose text color based on cell brightness
        normed = val / vmax
        text_color = "white" if normed > 0.55 else "black"
        weight = "bold" if on_diag else "normal"
        ax.text(j, i, str(val), ha="center", va="center",
                fontsize=8, color=text_color, fontweight=weight)

ax.set_xticks(range(len(x_labels)))
ax.set_xticklabels(x_labels, fontsize=9)
ax.set_yticks(range(len(y_labels)))
ax.set_yticklabels(y_labels, fontsize=9)
ax.set_xlabel("Predicted class", fontsize=10)
ax.set_ylabel("True class", fontsize=10)
ax.set_title("Confusion Matrix (10-class classifier)", fontsize=11, pad=10)

# Highlight diagonal with a thin border
for i in range(matrix.shape[0]):
    rect = plt.Rectangle((i - 0.5, i - 0.5), 1, 1,
                          linewidth=1.2, edgecolor="white", facecolor="none")
    ax.add_patch(rect)

plt.tight_layout()
fig.savefig("figure.pdf", dpi=300, bbox_inches="tight")
fig.savefig("figure.png", dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
fig.savefig("figure.svg", bbox_inches="tight")
print("Saved figure.pdf, figure.png, figure.svg")

content.text:
All five files are in place. Here's a summary of what was produced:

| File | Description |
|---|---|
| `figure.pdf` | Vector PDF, 44 KB |
| `figure.png` | Raster PNG at 150 dpi, 60 KB |
| `figure.svg` | SVG for further editing |
| `gen_figure.py` | Reproducible Python/Matplotlib script |
| `caption.tex` | `\caption{...}` ready to drop into a `figure` environment |

The heatmap uses the **Blues** sequential colormap for all cells. Diagonal entries (true positives, ranging 69–92) are annotated in bold with white text for readability on the darker cells; off-diagonal misclassifications use normal-weight text. Class C8 and C9 have the highest per-class accuracy (92 and 90 correct), while C1 has the lowest (69).

result:
All five files are in place. Here's a summary of what was produced:

| File | Description |
|---|---|
| `figure.pdf` | Vector PDF, 44 KB |
| `figure.png` | Raster PNG at 150 dpi, 60 KB |
| `figure.svg` | SVG for further editing |
| `gen_figure.py` | Reproducible Python/Matplotlib script |
| `caption.tex` | `\caption{...}` ready to drop into a `figure` environment |

The heatmap uses the **Blues** sequential colormap for all cells. Diagonal entries (true positives, ranging 69–92) are annotated in bold with white text for readability on the darker cells; off-diagonal misclassifications use normal-weight text. Class C8 and C9 have the highest per-class accuracy (92 and 90 correct), while C1 has the lowest (69).
